Q1: Honestly, I didn't really have a solid study routine. I'd tell myself I was going to study for like 30 hours a week, and sometimes I did sit there for that long, but a lot of it was just... existing near my laptop. I'd have the LMS open, maybe a video playing, but my mind would wander. I'd scroll through stuff without really absorbing it. The structure just wasn't there for me.
Q2: My pattern was all over the place. I'd have bursts where I'd cram before something was due, then long stretches of nothing. Deadlines were basically the only thing that got me moving. If there wasn't something due soon, I'd convince myself I had time and then just drift through the days doing other things or just zoning out.
Q3: Usually it was panic. Like, I'd suddenly remember an assignment was due or I'd get a notification on my phone. Sometimes I'd log in just to feel like I was doing something productive, even if I ended up just clicking around without purpose. The anxiety would spike and I'd think logging in would help calm it down, but it rarely did.
Q4: For a few weeks early on, I got into a rhythm where I'd check the LMS every morning with my coffee. It felt almost automatic. But then I missed a few days because of extracurriculars, and the habit just... evaporated. Once I broke the chain, getting back into it felt impossible. The routine never really stuck after that.
Q5: The lecture recordings were actually helpful because I learn better by listening. I could replay parts I didn't catch the first time. Discussion posts were okay too because hearing other people's takes sometimes made things click for me. But I had to be in the right headspace to actually benefit from them, which wasn't often.
Q6: The readings and PDFs, I basically ignored those. Reading walls of text just makes my brain shut off. I'd open them, scroll a bit, then close them. Same with some of the supplementary materials—they felt like extra work that wouldn't directly help my grade, so I kept putting them off until I just never did them.
Q7: If I could see a direct connection to passing an assignment or the exam, I'd engage with it. Like, if a video specifically covered something I knew would be tested, I'd watch it. But anything that felt vague or optional? My brain just categorized it as skippable. I needed that immediate payoff to feel motivated.
Q8: Optional stuff felt like a trap, honestly. Like, they say it's optional, but then you wonder if you're missing something important. But because it wasn't required, I'd always push it aside for 'later' and later never came. Required activities at least had the pressure of consequences, which was the only thing that sometimes got me moving.
Q9: Yeah, I'd download lecture slides and audio and just listen to them while doing other stuff, like walking to my extracurriculars or lying in bed. I also used YouTube videos on similar topics when the course material wasn't clicking. None of that would show up in the LMS, but it was still studying in my own way.
Q10: There were nights where I'd have my notes spread out, listening to downloaded lectures on repeat, trying to piece things together. Or I'd be talking through concepts with a friend over text. The LMS would show nothing for those hours, but I was genuinely trying to understand the material, even if it didn't always work.
Q11: Definitely. I'd watch a video once on the LMS, download the slides, and then never go back to the platform for that topic. I'd study from the downloaded stuff on my phone or laptop offline. It was easier than logging in every time, especially when my internet was being slow or I was somewhere without good wifi.
Q12: Around midterms, everything got harder. My anxiety was through the roof, and I felt paralyzed. I'd open the LMS and just stare at the screen, feeling overwhelmed by how much I'd fallen behind. The more I avoided it, the worse the anxiety got, which made me avoid it more. It was a brutal cycle.
Q13: Absolutely. When I had stuff going on with my extracurriculars or when I was just mentally exhausted, the LMS was the last thing I wanted to deal with. Confusion about what was actually due made it worse—I'd log in, feel lost, and log out feeling more stressed than before. Fatigue made everything feel impossible.
Q14: All the time. I'd have videos playing while I was basically dissociating, just watching the progress bar move. Or I'd click through discussion posts without really reading them. I was technically 'active' but my brain was somewhere else entirely. It was like going through the motions without any real engagement.
Q15: I postponed assignments constantly, even when I knew they mattered. The anxiety made starting feel impossible—like this huge mountain I couldn't climb. I'd tell myself I'd do it tomorrow, and tomorrow would come and I'd feel the same dread. It's why my assignment completion was so low despite actually caring about passing.
Q16: Deadlines were everything. They were the only thing that could cut through the fog and make me actually do something. I'd have these intense bursts of activity right before something was due, cramming everything into a few hours. It wasn't healthy, but without that pressure, I just couldn't make myself engage.
Q17: When I got feedback, it could go either way. Good feedback made me feel like maybe I could do this, which helped a little. But critical feedback sometimes sent me into a spiral where I'd avoid the course for days because I felt like a failure. It's complicated—feedback mattered, but my reaction to it was unpredictable.
Q18: The course structure was pretty open, which sounds nice but was actually hard for me. I needed more guidance, more check-ins, something to keep me on track. The freedom meant I could drift, and drift I did. A more rigid structure with smaller, frequent deadlines might have helped me stay engaged instead of falling behind.
Q19: Having so many online courses at once made it hard to keep track of everything. The LMS layout was fine, but when you're juggling eleven courses, it all blurs together. What would've helped is clearer priorities—like, 'do this first, this is most important.' Without that, I'd just freeze and do nothing.
Q20: That's literally me. I'd log in constantly, watch videos, click around, but then choke when it came to actually submitting work. It's the anxiety—engaging with content feels safer than putting yourself out there with an assignment that'll be judged. Activity doesn't equal output when you're paralyzed by fear of failure.
Q21: I can see that happening if someone studies mostly offline or just works really efficiently. Some people can read something once and get it. I'm not really that person, but I get how the LMS wouldn't capture someone who just downloads everything and studies independently. The platform only sees what you do on the platform.
Q22: Course logs can show when you're active and what you click on, but they can't show what's going on in your head. They can't tell if you're actually learning or just scrolling mindlessly. They miss the offline studying, the anxiety spirals, the times you're trying but failing to engage. It's just surface-level data.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring and understanding, not just showing up. It's when the material clicks and you want to learn more, not just pass. For me, real engagement was rare—those few moments when something actually interested me and I forgot about my anxiety. Most of the time I was just surviving, not engaging.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how much I was struggling internally. It wouldn't show the panic attacks before logging in, or the hours I spent worrying about falling behind. It wouldn't capture the CBT sessions I did to try to cope, or how my mood actually improved toward the end even though my grades didn't reflect that. The human stuff gets lost in the data.
